entries.short: The four squares in the middle of the board: e4, d4, e5, and d5.
Bringing your knights and bishops off the back row to active squares early in the game.
A single move thought of as time. You 'gain a tempo' when you make a useful move while also forcing your opponent to react.
A column of squares running up and down the board, named a through h.
A row of squares running across the board, numbered 1 through 8.
An attack on the king. The player in check must get out of it immediately.
Removing an opponent's piece by moving onto its square.
A move that, if you were allowed a second move, would win material or deliver checkmate.
One piece attacking two (or more) enemy pieces at the same time.
When a piece can't move because doing so would expose a more valuable piece behind it.
A square deep in the opponent's half where your knight sits safely, protected by a pawn.
A move or habit that stops the opponent's idea before it becomes a direct threat.
A pawn with no enemy pawn ahead of it on the same file or neighboring files, so it has a clearer path to promotion.
Putting a piece on the square directly in front of a pawn so that pawn cannot advance.
A file with no pawns on it, giving rooks and queens a clear vertical line.
The row just behind the opponent's pawns, where an invading rook can attack pawns and restrict the king.
A capture sequence where material leaves the board. Good exchanges improve your position; bad exchanges solve the opponent's problems.
A diagonal group of pawns defending each other. The front points toward space; the base is often the target.
Defending an important square or pawn more times than seems necessary, so your pieces coordinate around a strong point.
The useful freedom your pieces have. A mobile piece has meaningful squares and can join the plan quickly.
